DOLLAR INDEX SEASONAL TENDENCY AND KEY MOVEMENTS
If we have the Dollar Index as a seasonal tendency, should this be occurring at the same time that we see the June-July rally? Should that be occurring with a bullish or bearish move in the Dollar Index? Here's that seasonal tendency for the Dollar, and you can see around the January-February time there's usually a rally that takes place, which is contrary to what we saw in the 10-year treasury notes declining. Then we have a significant high forming between June and July, and then the Dollar Index typically trades down the rest of the year, usually making a low around the last week of October, first week of November, and it creates a small little bounce on November time period for the Dollar Index.

DOLLAR INDEX HISTORICAL MOVEMENT AND CURRENT EXPECTATIONS
We've observed a movement from the highs in October 2022, down to the sell side. We previously anticipated a move into a fair value gap (buyside imbalance, sell-side inefficiency) and an order block. If we had gone below a certain low, consequent encroachment (midpoint of the gap) was a potential target. The market structure shifted bullish, leading to a bullish breaker and subsequent consolidation around a higher order block. Price then rallied into a volume imbalance, traded down into it, and worked its way higher. We've been live, real-time, discussing these movements. My attention is now drawn to an area up ahead, and we are currently marching directly into it.

FAIR VALUE GAP DYNAMICS
A Fair Value Gap (FVG) was observed on the 15-minute timeframe. After running the buy side, the market drew down, took out sell side, and then overran this FVG, trading into a larger FVG. The rationale for this behavior is that if the market intends to trade above a smaller FVG (reaching the low end of a specific candle), it is likely to continue into a larger, more prominent FVG.

FRAMING MARKET LOGIC WITH DXY
All my analysis, across Forex, bond markets, equities, and index futures, starts with the Dollar Index. It frames the logic for whether I should assume risk (e.g., buying foreign currencies or stocks), which correlates with a lower dollar (risk-on). At the beginning of the year, we were indeed looking for a lower dollar, as documented in my YouTube analysis and Twitter commentary. Our prior analysis for DXY looked for lower prices and sell-side liquidity, which was absolutely tagged. If price went below that, we identified an order block and a fair value gap. This fair value gap is a buy-side imbalance sell-side inefficiency, occurring between a candle's high and a subsequent candle's low.

IDENTIFYING FAIR VALUE GAPS (FVG) AFTER DISPLACEMENT
After a swing high is taken out and an energetic break below it occurs: Look for a clear Fair Value Gap (FVG) formed by a smashing down movement. The low, the high, and if price trades back up into that FVG, you look for a short entry. Target the sell-side liquidity resting below the swing low identified prior to 8:30 AM.

FAIR VALUE GAP AS DYNAMIC SUPPORT
The five-minute Fair Value Gap is now above the current price level. In a correct bullish narrative, when price is above a Fair Value Gap, it tends to act as support. This behavior is contrasted with classic support and resistance, which 'hardly ever works'. The FVG is the expected mechanism for support in this scenario.

DETAILED EXPLANATION OF FAIR VALUE GAP (FVG)
A detailed explanation of the Fair Value Gap is provided, highlighting the mechanics of efficient versus Price started a rally, came up to a candle's high, and then repriced back down into a low. This portion is The concept is explained using a paint roller analogy: initially, paint is amply distributed, but as the roller moves higher, it runs out of paint, creating 'porous pockets' or areas of unequal distribution. The FVG is the area where paint (price) wasn't equally distributed, specifically a gap between one candle's high and the next candle after a large up-close candle, where nothing was offered back down to touch the prior candle's high. This signifies a lack of symmetric price delivery.

DXY FOUR-HOUR ANALYSIS
Reviewing the DXY four-hour chart, working with the old daily Fair Value Gap. Price traded up into two down-closed candles, forming a bullish order block. A small 'buy side imbalance sell side inefficiency' (BISI) exists, not noted on the chart for cleanliness, We have an order block and Fair Value Gap, where price traded up into, falling just short of the very high Even using the inefficiency shown, price didn't get to that either. A small measure of buy side liquidity was engaged on Friday, but continuation on the upside is expected.

This observation is part of early pre-market analysis, before the market officially opens, noting that while it touched the premium, it doesn't preclude further upward movement.

THE US DOLLAR INDEX AS A FOUNDATION
Every significant move in the Forex or currency markets has its roots in the price action of the US Dollar Index. A significant price move in a currency will be mirrored in the DXY. While the Dollar Index can consolidate on very short-term or intraday charts, and foreign currencies can still trade stronger or weaker against it, the search for Mega Trades fundamentally depends on the Dollar Index supporting the trade idea. Without DXY support, it is not a qualified Mega Trade setup.

TIMING AND FAIR VALUE GAPS
Gaps like these usually occur in the New York session, typically around 8:30 AM (when news embargoes lift), allowing more time for the market to unfold. If a gap occurs later, such as the 10 or 11 o'clock hour in New York, a portion of the gap may remain open. This remaining open portion would be classified as a fair value gap, to be potentially closed at a later time, but not necessarily during the specific trading day.

DXY DAILY CHART
Analyzing the daily chart inside the weekly decline that took out the sell side. A rally occurred into a buy side inefficiency, then traded down. Price rose, reusing an old Fair Value Gap (FVG), accumulating new long positions. It traded up into a Fair Value Sell Side and a Buy Side Imbalance (BISI). These are typical highs; the idea is for price to trade up into that area, but this changes if current lows are lost, leading to a move down to the old sell side level. Current sentiment on the dollar is bullish, expecting weakness in indices and continuation lower in Forex.

MARKET SYMMETRY, INTERMARKET ANALYSIS, AND BIAS CONFIRMATION
The Dollar Index (DXY) is coming off a short-term high and is not making a higher high. This lack of DXY confirmation indicates a deviation from 'Market Symmetry.' Market Symmetry: All related instruments (DXY, Forex, Equities) move in tandem, confirming a 'risk-on' or Risk-Off: DXY higher, Forex and Equities lower (easy short sales). Intermarket Analysis: Continuously measuring 'market breadth' across multiple assets (DXY, NQ, YM, ES, Forex) prevents reliance on a single asset to determine bias. This is vital for understanding bias, identifying high-probability conditions, and discerning 'fake runs.' ES Price Action for Smart Money: For ES to confirm Smart Money's intent to push higher, it needs to 'smash through' the teal FVG (4128 CE), 'rip right through it,' and ideally not retrace, which would be a 'signature' indicating the 41.36 level is targeted.

DXY WEEKLY CHART

Price previously worked up into this level several weeks ago. A liquidity pool for sell side liquidity is present. The acceleration or rejection at this level will determine if price targets lower sell side liquidity or remains within the range.

DOLLAR INDEX, COMMODITY CURRENCIES, AND MEASUREMENT TOOLS
If the US Dollar Index is moving higher, this will be seen with commodity currencies moving lower. If the US Dollar Index is moving down, it will lead to a commodity currency rally or movement higher. To measure this relationship, one can use the US Dollar Index versus the CRB Index (Commodity Research Bureau Index), available at crbtrader.com. More information will be provided in the PDF notes.

IDENTIFYING A FAIR VALUE GAP ON A DAILY CHART
Looking at a Euro Dollar daily chart, an example of a fair value gap is identified by a blue shaded area. This area, approximately a 20-pip range on the daily chart, is what is referred to as a fair value gap. Consider the candle to the left of the down candle that comprises the fair value gap.

OPTIMAL BEARISH FVG PLACEMENT & ENTRY
Optimal bearish ICT Fair Value Gaps are found after a run into buy-side liquidity (above an old high or multiple highs like a double top). This signifies smart money trading up to acquire short positions. When you see a run above an old high, followed by a breakdown, then look for this FVG pattern. This is the 'saving grace' pattern for smart money to get short if they missed the initial run above the high. Their stop loss would be placed above the high that initiated the FVG.

EXPECTED SYMMETRICAL BEHAVIOR
If the Dollar Index trades higher, it puts downward pressure on foreign currencies. If the Dollar Index trades lower, it allows foreign currencies to rally.

FVG AS A PROFITABLE CONDITION IN RANGE-BOUND MARKETS
While the FVG fill may not always appear to offer a large amount of pips, it represents a highly profitable When identifying fair value gaps alongside double tops (where buy stops rest above) and observing a 'turtle soup' run below an old low, especially in a range-bound, consolidating market (e.g., end-of-year holiday trading), this is the optimal trading style. This particular price swing, involving a fair value gap fill, yielded over 100 pips in approximately two days. The fair value gap, delineated on the daily chart, was successfully filled.

TESTING FVG FOR BUY-SIDE OBJECTIVE AND MORNING MOVE ANALYSIS

FAIR VALUE GAPS
In the PM session, after hitting the daily objective, the market shifts. First PM Buy Setup: Following a break in market structure to the upside, price retraces down into a mitigation block and an imbalance. This offers a buying opportunity, aiming for the PM buy-side liquidity pool (e.g., 4532 to 4540 level). Buying here allows selling into waiting buy stops at the target. Second PM Buy Setup: Price trades down to an old high (that was previously broken). When an old high is broken, it can act as a discount array and become support, especially when the overall logic suggests a move into a premium. This setup often coincides with filling an imbalance. First PM Sell Setup: Once the market fulfills the initial buy-side liquidity in the shaded pink area, it can do A break below a swing low, indicating a market structure shift. An imbalance (fair value gap) where price retraces before selling off. A seller can enter here, aiming for a Second PM Sell Setup: Following a retracement into a fair value gap, price breaks lower again, creating another imbalance. Even if price overshoots the first fair value gap, a second, smaller one nearby, or using the first as an entry, can be a valid short, again targeting an order block or low.

FAIR VALUE GAPS (FVG) / SIBI
Transitioning from a line chart (which only notes closing prices) to OHLC candlesticks provides more detail, showing the highest high, lowest low, open, and close of each interval. The same conceptual idea applies: relative equal highs are attacked, followed by sell side delivery. An inefficiency is demonstrated by a Fair Value Gap (FVG). For a down-closed candle, an FVG forms when there's a separation between the previous candle's low and the next candle's high, with only the middle candle creating that void. This is classified as a SIBI (Sell Side Imbalance, Buy Side Inefficiency). The market's algorithm will typically trade back up into this FVG to 'patch' or fill the inefficiency, striving for an efficient market. The bodies of the candles provide the narrative, indicating respect for these levels.

MARKET EFFICIENCY PARADIGM & FVG IMPLICATIONS
When price trades back into a bearish FVG, and you are bearish, it serves as a short signal, with the expectation of a continued move lower. Viewing price action through this Market Efficiency Paradigm, looking for this characteristic, will unlock many insights and change your perspective on charts.

THE IMPORTANCE OF FAIR VALUE GAPS FOR PRICE MOVEMENT
The reason why this is important is because there was no up candle or up movement between the break of that low and the high of this candle here; it was all just straight down movement, so nothing filled in in this area. Once price broke this low, it left it open, basically it's like a gap. The same thing occurs here: when this up candle is broken here on this candle, once it started breaking lower, there's a gap between this candle's low and this candle body or wick. I define it by the body; I like to use the range in which it causes this void. When price is below that, this is going to be fair value. The market's going to want to come back to that because there was very little trading in there, just like we said, there was a gap because there was no movement up in this area here between this up candle's low and this up candle's high. This area in price action only saw down movement; didn't have any up candle movement, only down movement. All this down here is down candle price action only, big range, so this is a liquidity void. The fact that it creates it in big ranges and speed, that's what defines it. Now, because price moves so quickly in these areas, fair value is established because there's going to be a willingness to want to see price trade back up into these levels in here and close in all this. In other words, there's going to be up movement later on. It won't happen immediately always; sometimes it takes a little bit of time, sometimes depending upon the time frame you're looking at, it may take a great deal of time. But when price starts to move high, we know that it will try to trade into this range here and fill that in at a later time. That is where market makers view fair value.

IMPACT ON EXPORTS, STOCKS, AND BONDS
When examining commodities, grains and agricultural products are highly export-sensitive: A strong dollar diminishes demand for grain and livestock/agricultural exports (grains and meats). US Dollar Index weakness instills increased demand for grain and agricultural exports. The US Dollar Index also affects stocks and bonds: If the US Dollar Index is going higher or rallying, this is seen with stocks and bonds moving up, as it is supportive of the stock and bond market going higher. If the US Dollar Index moves lower, this is seen with stocks and bonds both trending lower as well.

BEARISH FVG ANNOTATIONS AND TRADE ENTRY
The range between Candle 1's low and Candle 3's high is the FVG. Easiest Entry: A limit order just above Candle 3's high (e.g., one tick above). Stop Loss: Above Candle 1 or above Candle 2. For learners, a wider stop (above Candle 1) is recommended initially to allow for range. Experienced traders can reduce this stop.

DOLLAR INDEX & INVERSE CORRELATIONS
For smart money distribution for sell programs, focusing on manipulation in the underlying versus The The dollar Index makes a higher high at the same time the dollar Swiss would be making a lower high. That would be relative weakness in the dollar Swiss pair, with the dollar Index's move above an old high being a run on buy stops for a dollar, followed by rejection and lower prices on the dollar Index. The dollar Index and the dollar Swiss are closely, positively correlated, just like dollar CAD (when oil is not an issue) and dollar yen are also closely, positively correlated to the dollar Index. Versus the euro dollar and the dollar Index, they're inversely correlated, meaning whatever the euro dollar is doing, the opposite is seen in the dollar Index. In a sell program example, if the euro dollar makes a higher high while the dollar Index makes a higher low, this indicates relative strength in the dollar Index and underlying weakness should be expected after euro dollar takes out that old high, running out buy stop liquidity, and the daily chart should see lower If the dollar Index makes a lower low while the euro dollar makes a lower high, that's relative weakness in the euro dollar pair. While the dollar Index goes below that lower low, it would be scooping up sell side liquidity, followed by rejection and trading higher on the dollar Index, thus pushing euro dollar lower.
